For `nan` vs exception, I found the most efficient workflow for featurizing a lot (all ~60k MP mats) of (potentially) error prone data was to ignore_errors = True and filter mostly nan features with pandas nan threshold afterwards. But I can also see why throwing exceptions is good, in the case of accidentally putting in a structure for a comp or something, because if you had ignore_errors on, you would be essentially eliminating a good feature and not even know it.

Maybe in the future we can throw a warning to the user for each feature that has 90+% (or some parameter percentage) nan values. That way, even if you have a df with 300+ columns, you might not see which columns are mostly nan (and possibly due to a user error) unless you explicitly check, but you would get a warning saying "Look at feature X to make sure all these nan values are actually due to some crazy error, not due to accidentally putting in an invalid argument.

With your modifications to adapt, likely things would match when cased correctly, and fail when cased incorrectly (think Band Name vs band name). Your use case seems pretty specific, so this may not matter to you. There may be room for a case-sensitive mode inside adapt, though tokenization (which would remove / characters) is something I'll still have to think about. 

Not all text sent to adapt via recognizer has the same case; it's inconsistent from recognizer to recognizer (IBM tries to be smarter with capitalization and punctuation for example, whereas google doesn't really try hard). For most command and control scenarios, case insensitive (and generally fuzzy) matching is preferable. If you're talking about literal unix commands, aliases and search within your skill will likely provide a better user experience (via voice), but again your use cases may differ.


Adapt is intended for natural language input, and things like literal file names don't (at least in my opinion) fall under that purview. I'm happy to see if we can make adapt work for your use case, but if I have to choose between this use case and general adapt quality/usability, I'll favor the latter.

The [NIFTI format](https://nifti.nimh.nih.gov/pub/dist/src/niftilib/nifti1.h) is very explicit about this. Specifically `dimensions  1,2,3 for space (x,y,z), 4 for time (t), and 5,6,7 for anything else needed.` Therefore, if you look at a 2D image across time fslhd will report dims 1..4 as X,Y,1,T. On the other hand, a 3D volume across time (e.g. fMRI time series) will be X,Y,Z,T. In theory, diffusion datasets with different gradient directions (D) should be saved as X,Y,Z,1,D. In practice, the available tools save diffusion data as X,Y,Z,D (a violation of the strict NIfTI definitions). Another issue is how to save images with multiple echoes or data where each channel of the coil saves a different spatial image. One option is to use the dimensions 5..7 to encode this. This is valid, but most tools do not know how to distinguish more than 4 dimensions in one file. For [dcm2niix](https://github.com/rordenlab/dcm2niix) I save each of these as a separate file (e.g. "e2" means 2nd echo and "c2" means second channel).

This is real-world example of how to use lists/arrays as input values: Basically the same behavior of the `node` field, but get a list of Relay nodes for a list of Relay IDs:

```python
import graphene
from graphene import relay

class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
    node = relay.Node.Field(required=True)
    nodes = graphene.List(relay.Node, required=True, ids=graphene.List(graphene.ID))

    def resolve_nodes(self, info, ids=None):
        if ids:
            return [
                relay.Node.get_node_from_global_id(info, node_id)
                for node_id in ids
            ]
        return []
```

Thanks for this PR!  Would be great to finally enable this functionality.  However, I don't think `otPlatSecureRandomGet()` can/should rely on a pseudorandom number generator.  The LFSR on the CC2538 is only 16 bits, which means that relying on that LFSR only really gives about 16 bits of entropy.  `otPlatSecureRandomGet()` function should provide true random values for whatever size is requested.

> Also I have a question regarding Mle::BecomeChild function. Why is the initial MLE Parent Request delayed by 1000 ms? Is this a overlook, or are there any implementation-specific reasons for that?

I don't think there's any reason it should be delayed by a fixed value.  While the Thread Spec does not indicate delaying by a random value, there might be reason to do so (e.g. in the case of merging partitions to avoid a large number of nodes responding to an MLE Advertisement at the same time).  Thoughts?

Oh wow, I didn't realize that **do_save()** was using ``pyparsing`` for its parsing logic. 

Everything should be migrated to use the argparse-based decorator for parsing if it needs special parsing or perhaps just the decorator which causes a list of arguments to get passed if it doesn't need special parsing but needs more than a single argument.  Some of the existing functions which use **@options**, such as **do_pyscript()** were using it with an empty option list just so they could get a list of argument strings intstead of a single string - i.e. to take advantage of the shlex parsing provided by the **@options** decorator.

The custom argument parsing in **do_set()** is pretty non-standard in terms of the way it tries hard to cast anything into the type expected for the given setting, so I wouldn't recommend attempting to port that to an argparse-based solution.

OK, I've tracked this down to what appears to be an Amazon SES bug: if you are using open or click tracking, when SES rewrites your message to add the tracking pixels/links, it mangles any `Content-Transfer-Encoding: 8bit` text parts.

Here are two immediate workarounds:
* If you don't need open or click tracking, don't enable these options in your SES configuration set. (SES won't rewrite your message, and everything else will work correctly.)
* If you *do* need open or click tracking, ensure that *both* the plaintext and html message bodies have at least one line longer than 998 characters—it can be a line of spaces or an HTML comment. (The long lines trick Django into forcing `Content-Transfer-Encoding: quoted-printable`, and SES's message rewriting handles this correctly.)

I *think* I've also found a way to work around the SES bug in Anymail's backend, but it will require more testing. (I'll report the bug to Amazon first; obviously it would be preferable for them to fix SES.)

Hello Jason,

thank you for looking into this -- 2.9% starts to sound like a more reasonable number. I did a quick pass over your code and think that most changes are quite reasonable (e.g. recording the type in all cases when ``descr<>`` is constructed).

One part gives me a bit of a headache though, and that's the stack of implicit conversions you added. The two things that bother me about it are:

1. This is a massive complication of the function dispatcher and other parts of the library. Only the dispatcher ever pushes stuff on it, so I wonder: why can't the normal stack be used for this via standard recursive function calls? (i.e. a local variable in the dispatcher)

2. This is state that is very much global -- not just within a module but also across different Python threads and completely different extension modules built with pybind11. The ``internals`` data structure is shared by all of them, and the implications of this are not clear to me.

Thus it might be good to consider if some other (simpler) designs are feasible here.

Thanks,
Wenzel

As far as I can see, we're down to two proposals:

1. Have `operator()` with unsafe access by default + `at()`/`mutable_at()` for safe access.
2. @aldanor's proposal from https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/pull/617#issuecomment-275548016 where `py::array` is safe by default, but can return a super simple array (not `py::object` derived) with unsafe access (e.g. [quick sketch](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/pull/617#issuecomment-275558709)).

I'm personally in favor of option 2. What about other?

There may be two issues to resolve here:
(1) If you have a proper singleton, then you should perhaps be wrapping a method that returns `const T*` in lieu of `unique_ptr<T>` or `unique_ptr<const T>`, since the actual object is owned via the `Singleton2::instance()` method.
(2) `pybind11` (and Python in general) do not have the intrinsic ability to provide `const` views into an object. See https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/issues/717 for some discussion on it.

(1) is your main blocker. To bind a singleton, you should do something like this:
```
py:::class_<Singleton>  // We don't care about the holder, because Python will never own this instance.
   // Do not define `init`; if you can't construct the class publicly in C++, you shouldn't construct it in Python.
   .def("instance", &Singleton::instance, py::return_value_policy::reference);  // This returns a pointer to the singleton; it's lifetime is dictated by `Singleton::instance()`
```

Be careful on lifetime; consider using something like `never_destroyed` if the destruction can do bad things:
http://drake.mit.edu/styleguide/cppguide.html#Static_and_Global_Variables

(2) shouldn't be that much of a problem; just be careful on what you bind and expose to Python, and you should be good to go. If you *really* need fanciful `const` views, you'll need to implement your own mechanism, either following the suggestions in #717 or something else.

Because * and ** are what reStructuredText uses for italics and boldness. The usual is to do something like this, which also has the advantage of formatting the code in monospace:

```
"""Context manager for running code that must be cleaned up on failure.
The context manager allows you to register functions that will be called
when an exception (excluding SystemExit) or signal is encountered. Usage::

    handler = ErrorHandler(cleanup1_func, *cleanup1_args, **cleanup1_kwargs)
    handler.register(cleanup2_func, *cleanup2_args, **cleanup2_kwargs)

with handler::

    do_something()

Or for one cleanup function::

    with ErrorHandler(func, args, kwargs):
        do_something()

If an...
"""
```

Note the double colons, which render the following indented paragraph in monospace. If you want inline monospace, that's done by surrounding text with double backticks.

                        obtained/renewed. When renewing several certificates
                        that have identical pre-hooks, only the first will be
                        executed. (default: None)
  --post-hook POST_HOOK
                        Command to be run in a shell after attempting to
                        obtain/renew certificates. Can be used to deploy
                        renewed certificates, or to restart any servers that
                        were stopped by --pre-hook. This is only run if an
                        attempt was made to obtain/renew a certificate. If
                        multiple renewed certificates have identical post-

`wp load` is unlikely to work when given on the command-line; in fact, nothing that requires you to be connected to the vehicle is likely to work, including things like mode changes.

mavlink is a lossy protocol; when you do these commands it is sending a message into the void and hoping for a response.  Since no vehicle is connected, the packet is lost.

Even from the command-line, a `wp load` is not guaranteed to work.  If the first packet is lost it will just fail silently (and we 've seen that happen on real aircraft).

The "loaded waypoints" message refers only to MAVProxy successfully parsing the supplied file.  We could add a "Sending waypoint count" message to the user, but that would be getting somewhat verbose, I think.

I think it might make more sense in OAuthenticator, since there's no
equivalent of nss_ldap for use there. I don't think we should have that
functionality in the core ldapauthenticator module. I *will* work on a doc
for using nss_ldap though. I'll also be happy to CR the separate module if
you want me to.

To recap,

1. This functionality is already provided by the battle tested pam_ldap +
nss_ldap modules
2. Doing this 'right' is pretty complex, since you need to figure out how
to handle deletions, changes in username / attributes, etc. pamd_ldap +
nss_ldap deal with all of this for us.
3. While setting up pam_ldap + nss_ldap requires root or sudo privs, so
does useradd.
4. This is a very common use case, so I'll add documentation making this
clearer.
5. OAuthenticator does include this functionality, which I think is ok
because there's no equivalent of nss_ldap for oauth. I also note that not
all of them provide this - the Mediawiki one, for example. It would be
fairly complex in that case, since MediaWiki usernames can contain
characters that are not permitted in unix accounts.

Thank you for talking it through, and I hope I have been able to
communicate my reasons clearly. Looking forward to helping out the new
module in any way I can :)

@iandees looks like this won't work for you _exactly_ (python 2 vs 3), but for reference here's a known-working `Dockerfile` for using this C++ . The `python:3.5.2` base image is Debian Jessie, so similar should be achievable on Ubuntu.

```
FROM python:3.5.2

RUN mkdir -p /provisioning
WORKDIR /provisioning

# Install OS dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
      build-essential \
      curl \
      unzip && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Get libgeos for Python Shapely package
RUN mkdir -p /provisioning/geos && \
    cd /provisioning/geos && \
    curl -# -O http://download.osgeo.org/geos/geos-3.5.0.tar.bz2 && \
    tar -xjf geos-3.5.0.tar.bz2 && \
    cd geos-3.5.0 && \
    ./configure && \
    make -j$(python -c 'import multiprocessing; print(multiprocessing.cpu_count())') && \
    make -j$(python -c 'import multiprocessing; print(multiprocessing.cpu_count())') install && \
    ldconfig -v && \
    rm -rf /provisioning/geos

# Get the protobuf package and build from source
# https://github.com/google/protobuf/releases
RUN mkdir -p /provisioning/protobuf && \
    cd /provisioning/protobuf && \
    curl -# -L -O https://github.com/google/protobuf/archive/v3.1.0-alpha-1.tar.gz && \
    tar -xvf v3.1.0-alpha-1.tar.gz && \
    cd protobuf-3.1.0-alpha-1 && \
    ./autogen.sh && \
    ./configure && \
    make -j$(python -c 'import multiprocessing; print(multiprocessing.cpu_count())') && \
    make -j$(python -c 'import multiprocessing; print(multiprocessing.cpu_count())') install && \
    cd python && \
    ldconfig && \
    python setup.py install --cpp_implementation && \
    rm -rf /provisioning/protobuf

# Install mapbox-vector-tile directly from the git repo
RUN mkdir -p cd /provisioning/mapbox-vector-tile && \
    cd /provisioning/mapbox-vector-tile && \
    curl -# -L -O https://github.com/tilezen/mapbox-vector-tile/archive/v1.0.0.tar.gz && \
    curl -# -L -o 6e3a0c7.tar.gz https://github.com/tilezen/mapbox-vector-tile/tarball/6e3a0c751c515bef66bd72a36dcd8cf41fa63fe8 && \
    tar -xf 6e3a0c7.tar.gz && \
    cd tilezen-mapbox-vector-tile-6e3a0c7 && \
    python setup.py install && \
    rm -rf /provisioning/mapbox-vector-tile
```

### String interpolation

1. One of the biggest differences between SoS and Python (or extra syntax)
2. The use of `${}`
3. Point 1: Applicable to `" "`, not `' '`
4. Point 2: The usual Python `format` syntax (`:`)
5. Point 3: The script format is `""" """` that subject to interpolation.
6. Point 4: Converters
  * Manipulation of paths
  * Handling of arrays and dictionaries (which is quite different from Python 3.6 format string syntax)

I have now resolved this problem; it was never a streamlink problem in the first place.

To expand on how I figured it out in case anyone has this error, it was indeed a proxy problem as the error description suggested `Caused by ProxyError('Cannot connect to proxy.', RemoteDisconnected('Remote end closed connection without response',))`

After reading that this could possibly be arising [due to HTTPS connections](https://stackoverflow.com/a/33226080) I decided to debug my way line by line through the BBC iPlayer plugin, and sure enough it was the `http.get` request on Line 110 that was throwing the error - the only line (as far as I can tell) that uses a https connection in the plugin.

At first I tried modifying the request to include useragent as the aforementioned stack answer suggested, but that had no effect.

I decided to look again at my proxy settings and realised that for NordVPN I was using a http url for standard proxy and https url for the secure proxy. It was then I realised that I'm using a regular NordVPN proxy (for greater speed), not a secure one. I changed the https-proxy url to a standard http address and sure enough, the problem went away.

So the lesson here is partly that if you receive this error its probably because of your https-proxy settings, but mostly that you shouldn't doubt the streamlink team!

Thanks guys.

I don't know how to do this with ffmpeg in one go while having a proper video/audio container. This is a question you need to ask elsewhere, as it's a ffmpeg related question with file inputs, stdin inputs and writing to the same input file again. Also, as I've said, this is highly dependent on the input formats.

If you're recording HLS streams, then you can simply concatenate all MPEG-TS segments (which is what streamlink is doing, anyway). This will result in a file with broken timestamps though, so you need to fix it with ffmpeg afterwards. I'm remuxing it here to mp4, because writing the input file is not possible.

```bash
# append the new concatenated MPEG-TS segments to the previous recording
streamlink --stdout URL QUALITY >> recording.ts
# remux to mp4 (and fix)
ffmpeg -i recording.ts -c copy recording.mp4
```

That's all I can do to help you.

As a newcomer to ipywidgets, I originally misunderstood this. It's not that the last front-end widget forces itself onto the kernel widget: a many-to-one relation between front-end widgets and kernel widgets is maintained. So I kinda just ran into this. I have a parent and a bunch of children (ordered).

Naively I tried:

```py
def init_start(self):
    init_things  # get things started

async def wait_to_finish_work(self):
    await do_things

async def start(self):
    self.init_start()
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        for child in self.children:
            nursery.spawn(self.start)
        nursery.spawn(self.wait_to_finish_work)

>>> await root.start()
```

I was hoping that the children would spawn deterministically in order and at least run until the first await in `wait_to_finish_work`. This would start the tree in **depth first order**. However, as I noticed in the result and in the design above, the initial spawning is also not in order.

Specifically, you mention that newly spawned tasks may inherit their parent's `vtime`. I wonder if it may not be better that the nursery initially *birth* its children and start their life until the first suspension in the deterministic order. Spawn *looks* deterministic and the docs don't really say much either way.

The alternative for a case like mine where you want to start off each spawnee in order in current trio would be to re-write my example as:

```py
def init_start(self):
    init_things  # get things started

async def wait_to_finish_work(self):
    await do_things

async def start(self):
    for child in self.children:
        child.init_start()
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        for child in self.children:
            nursery.spawn(self.start)
        nursery.spawn(self.wait_to_finish_work)

>>> root.init_start()
>>> await root.start()
```
The second version is not that different, but it does make it more complex, especially for cleanup as the parent starts it in two methods. But also, this is now **breadth first order**, where at each level a node  is picked randomly to start off its children.

The tricky bit with the "forgetting to call `await`" is passing an async function around as any old object (including through a return statement) is a totally legitimate thing to do. About the only thing I can think of is if you call an async function and **don't** store its value then you obviously screwed up since you won't even get side-effects for that and so you could have the interpreter raise a warning/exception at that point instead of at destruction time of the object (which should get you the proper call line in the traceback and will work immediately in e.g. PyPy when they don't use refcounting).

The issue in this case is how to reliably detect that an unstored async function was never started (i.e. is having `POP_TOP` check whether an async function has started good enough to catch this situation **and** not have any false-positives?).
